did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture (Inception-v4) to evaluate the diagnostic performance for melanoma recognition in dermoscopic images. The CNN was trained on over 120,000 dermoscopic images and used to classify lesions as benign nevi or melanomas, calculating sensitivity, specificity, and ROC AUC.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Investigation' in JAMA Dermatology. It describes a cross-sectional study design where the authors conducted original experiments involving the acquisition of dermoscopic images with and without surgical skin markings, applied a deep learning CNN to these images, and reported new primary results regarding diagnostic performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC).

title: Association Between Surgical Skin Markings in Dermoscopic Images and Diagnostic Performance of a Deep Learning Convolutional Neural Network for Melanoma Recognition
brief_summary: This cross-sectional study investigated the impact of gentian violet surgical skin markings on the diagnostic performance of a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) for melanoma recognition. The CNN, an Inception-v4 architecture trained on over 120,000 dermoscopic images, was evaluated on 130 melanocytic lesions (107 benign nevi and 23 melanomas) in three conditions: unmarked, marked, and cropped. The results showed that skin markings significantly reduced specificity from 84.1% to 45.8% by increasing false-positive rates, likely due to the CNN associating the blue markings with melanoma. Conversely, cropping images to remove markings improved specificity to 97.2% and achieved the highest ROC AUC of 0.993. The study concludes that surgical skin markings interfere with CNN diagnosis and should be avoided in dermoscopic images intended for automated analysis.

lesion_types_studied.details: 23 melanocytic lesions diagnosed as melanoma (18 invasive, 4 in situ). Subtypes included superficial spreading melanoma (15), lentigo maligna melanoma (2), nodular melanoma (1), and acrolentiginous melanoma (1). Used as the positive class for diagnostic classification.
107 benign nevi included in the study dataset. Diagnosed based on absence of melanoma-associated features and uneventful follow-up. Used as the negative class for diagnostic classification.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic classification of melanocytic lesions into benign nevi or malignant melanoma
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task where the CNN predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant). The model outputs a malignancy probability score between 0 and 1, with a validated a priori cutoff greater than 0.5 used for dichotomous classification. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and ROC AUC.

datasets_used.name: Moleanalyzer-Pro Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: The dataset used to train the Inception-v4 convolutional neural network (CNN). It consists of dermoscopic images of skin neoplasms and corresponding diagnoses, provided by FotoFinder Systems GmbH.

use.training: Used to train the Inception-v4 CNN architecture.
use.other: The CNN was approved for use as a medical device in the European market based on this training.

datasets_used.name: Study Cohort (Heidelberg)
datasets_used.description: A cross-sectional cohort of 130 melanocytic lesions imaged at the Department of Dermatology, University of Heidelberg. The cohort includes benign nevi diagnosed via clinical/dermoscopic features and follow-up, and melanomas validated by histopathology. Images were acquired in three conditions: unmarked, marked with gentian violet, and cropped.

use.testing: Used as the test set to evaluate the diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, AUC) of the pre-trained CNN under three image conditions (unmarked, marked, cropped).
use.other: The benign nevi were acquired prospectively in clinical routine; melanoma images were randomly selected from the department's image library.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Database
datasets_used.description: An open-access database of dermoscopic images reviewed by the authors to assess the prevalence of skin markings in training data sources.

use.other: Used for secondary analysis to estimate the percentage of images containing skin markings (2.4% of melanomas and 2.3% of nevi) to hypothesize about potential biases in the CNN's training data.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the differences in melanoma probability scores between image sets (e.g., unmarked vs marked, in vivo vs electronically superimposed markings) and to compare benign lesions with in situ or invasive melanomas.
To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the CNN by calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for unmarked, marked, and cropped image sets.
To estimate the uncertainty for sensitivity, specificity, mean melanoma probability scores, and AUC values.

statistical_methods_used.method: Descriptive statistics (frequency, mean, range, standard deviation)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize the distribution of CNN melanoma probability scores and other metrics.
To compare sensitivities and specificities between different image conditions (unmarked, marked, cropped) to identify significant differences.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the distribution of melanoma probability scores between the compared groups (e.g., unmarked vs marked benign nevi, or benign nevi vs melanomas).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the distribution of melanoma probability scores between the compared groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study reported P-values to determine statistical significance. Specifically, it found that skin markings significantly increased melanoma probability scores in benign nevi (P < .001) and significantly reduced specificity (P < .001). It also found no significant difference between in vivo and electronically superimposed markings (P = .10) and no significant difference in sensitivity across image sets. The threshold for significance was implicitly 0.05, as P < .001 and P = .003 were deemed significant.

Study Cohort (Heidelberg).description: Cross-sectional cohort of 130 melanocytic lesions (107 benign nevi, 23 melanomas) evaluated under three image conditions: unmarked, marked with gentian violet, and cropped.

Electronic Superimposition Sub-study.description: Comparison of in vivo markings vs electronically superimposed markings on 20 benign nevi

Study Cohort (Heidelberg).notes: Skin markings significantly increased melanoma probability scores in benign nevi and reduced specificity. Cropping images to remove markings improved specificity and AUC. Heat maps indicated blue markings were associated with increased false-positive rates.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on more than 120,000 dermoscopic images of skin neoplasms and corresponding diagnoses (Moleanalyzer-Pro; FotoFinder Systems GmbH). Approved for use as a medical device in the European market.

architecture_details.implementation_details: Composed of many sequential layers assessing input images on a pixel level for 'good representations' (dermoscopic features). Heat maps were generated using vanilla gradient descent backpropagation to visualize relevant pixels.

training_details.labels: Corresponding diagnoses (skin neoplasms, benign nevi, melanoma)

classification_or_output_head.cutoff: Validated a priori cutoff greater than 0.5 for dichotomous classification

preprocessing_techniques: Manual cropping of dermoscopic images to remove surgical skin markings and focus on the melanocytic lesion
Digital superimposition of skin markings onto melanoma images using photograph manipulation software (Photoshop CS6, version 13.0.1 x32; Adobe Inc)
Generation of heat maps using vanilla gradient descent backpropagation to visualize relevant pixels for the CNN's prediction

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were manually cropped to remove surgical skin markings and focus on the melanocytic lesion. This was used as a post-processing step to evaluate the impact of removing artifacts on diagnostic performance, rather than a training augmentation technique.

Skin markings were digitally superimposed onto melanoma images using photograph manipulation software (Photoshop CS6, version 13.0.1 x32; Adobe Inc) to simulate in vivo markings for comparative analysis.

conclusions: Surgical skin markings (gentian violet) significantly interfere with the diagnostic performance of a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) for melanoma recognition.

Skin markings significantly increased the melanoma probability scores for benign nevi, leading to a substantial reduction in specificity (from 84.1% to 45.8%) and an increase in the false-positive rate.
The CNN likely associated the blue surgical skin markings with melanoma, potentially due to a predominance of such markings in the melanoma training images.
Cropping dermoscopic images to remove skin markings improved diagnostic performance, resulting in the highest specificity (97.2%) and area under the curve (0.993).
The study recommends avoiding surgical skin markings in dermoscopic images intended for analysis by a deep learning CNN to prevent misdiagnosis and unnecessary excisions.
important_remarks: The study cohort is limited to 130 lesions (107 benign nevi, 23 melanomas) from a single center (University of Heidelberg), with benign nevi diagnosed via follow-up rather than histopathology, limiting generalizability.
The population is biased toward fair-skinned patients in Germany; findings may not generalize to other skin types or genetic backgrounds.

The CNN (Inception-v4) was pre-trained on over 120,000 images from the Moleanalyzer-Pro dataset, which is a commercial medical device; the specific composition and bias of this training data were not fully disclosed, though an analysis of the ISIC database suggested a potential imbalance in skin marking prevalence.
The study demonstrates a significant drop in specificity (from 84.1% to 45.8%) when surgical skin markings are present, indicating a critical vulnerability of the model to common clinical artifacts.

The mechanism of the CNN's association between blue markings and melanoma is unclear; heat maps suggest the model relies on the markings rather than lesion features, highlighting a lack of transparency in the deep learning decision process.

Electronic superimposition of markings on melanoma images yielded similar results to in vivo markings, validating the experimental approach but also confirming that the artifact itself drives the error, not the physical act of marking.

Cropping images to remove markings significantly improved performance (AUC 0.993, specificity 97.2%), suggesting that simple preprocessing can mitigate this specific failure mode, but manual cropping may not be feasible in all clinical workflows.
